definition: The practice of refraining from some or all sexual activity. Can be temporary or long-term, and may be chosen for personal, health, or religious reasons.
The practice of attending to one another's physical and emotional needs after intimate activity. May include cuddling, hydration, verbal reassurance, or simply being present together.
The study of the structure of the body and its parts. Understanding intimate anatomy helps partners communicate about pleasure and health more effectively.
The opening at the end of the digestive tract. Contains many nerve endings and can be a source of pleasure. Requires specific safety considerations including lubrication and hygiene.

A substance believed to increase sexual desire. While many foods and supplements are marketed as aphrodisiacs, scientific evidence for most is limited. Psychological expectation often plays the biggest role.
The physiological and psychological state of sexual excitement. Involves increased blood flow to erogenous zones, elevated heart rate, and heightened sensitivity. Can be spontaneous or responsive.
A sexual orientation characterized by a persistent lack of sexual attraction toward any gender. Asexual individuals may still experience romantic attraction and desire emotional intimacy.
A pattern of relating to others in intimate relationships, shaped primarily by early childhood experiences. The four main styles are secure, anxious, avoidant, and disorganized.

A common vaginal condition caused by an imbalance of naturally occurring bacteria. Symptoms may include unusual discharge and odor. Treated with antibiotics. Not classified as an STI but can be influenced by intimate activity.
A form of contraception that physically prevents sperm from reaching an egg. Includes external condoms, internal condoms, dental dams, and diaphragms. Many also reduce STI transmission risk.
A sexual orientation characterized by attraction to people of one's own gender and other genders. The degree and nature of attraction may vary and can be fluid over time.
A person's perception and feelings about their own body. Negative body image is a significant barrier to sexual satisfaction and can lead to self-consciousness during intimate moments.
The practice of restraining a partner for erotic pleasure. Part of BDSM practices. Requires explicit consent, clear communication, safety protocols, and agreed-upon safe words.
Personal limits that define what feels comfortable and safe in a relationship. Boundaries can be physical, emotional, or sexual. They should be communicated clearly and respected unconditionally.
The voluntary abstention from sexual activity, typically for a sustained period. Different from asexuality in that it is a behavioral choice rather than an orientation.
Voluntary, enthusiastic, informed agreement to participate in a specific sexual activity. Must be freely given, reversible at any time, informed, enthusiastic, and specific to each activity.

A highly sensitive organ located at the front junction of the labia. Contains approximately 8,000 nerve endings. The visible external portion is only a small part of the full internal structure, which extends several centimeters inside the body.
A barrier device used during intercourse to reduce the risk of pregnancy and STI transmission. Available in external (worn on the penis) and internal (inserted into the vagina or anus) varieties.

Methods or devices used to prevent pregnancy. Options include barrier methods, hormonal methods, IUDs, implants, and permanent surgical procedures. Effectiveness varies by method and usage consistency.

A stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands. Elevated cortisol levels suppress sex hormones, reduce desire, and impair arousal. Chronic stress keeps cortisol high, negatively impacting intimate life.
Oral stimulation of the vulva and clitoris. An important form of intimate contact that many people find highly pleasurable. Communication about preferences enhances the experience for both partners.
A thin sheet of latex or polyurethane used as a barrier during oral-vulvar or oral-anal contact. Reduces the risk of STI transmission. Can be improvised by cutting open a condom.
The motivation or drive to engage in sexual activity. Can be spontaneous (arising without external stimuli) or responsive (emerging in response to arousing stimuli). Both types are normal.
A dome-shaped barrier contraceptive device inserted into the vagina before intercourse. Must be used with spermicide. Requires fitting by a healthcare provider.
A neurotransmitter associated with pleasure, reward, and motivation. Released during sexual activity and orgasm. Plays a key role in desire, attraction, and the brain's reward system.

Persistent or recurrent pain during sexual intercourse. Can affect any gender. Causes include insufficient lubrication, infections, pelvic floor dysfunction, endometriosis, and psychological factors. Treatable with medical support.
The practice of bringing oneself or a partner close to orgasm and then reducing stimulation before climax. Can intensify eventual orgasm and build arousal awareness.
Natural pain-relieving chemicals produced by the body. Released during exercise, laughter, and sexual activity. Contribute to feelings of well-being and pleasure during and after intimacy.

An area of the body with heightened sensitivity that can produce pleasurable sensations when stimulated. Primary zones include genitals and nipples. Secondary zones include neck, inner thighs, ears, and many others.

The persistent inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for sexual activity. Affects approximately 30 million men in the US. Often has physical causes (cardiovascular, hormonal) and is highly treatable.
A group of hormones that play a key role in reproductive and sexual health. Affects lubrication, tissue elasticity, mood, and desire. Levels fluctuate with menstrual cycle, pregnancy, and menopause.
Arousal from being observed by others during intimate activity. When practiced consensually between adults in appropriate settings, it is a normal variation of human sexuality.
Oral stimulation of the penis. An intimate act that many people find pleasurable. Communication about preferences, boundaries, and STI protection enhances safety and enjoyment.

A strong sexual fixation on a specific object, body part, or situation that is a primary source of arousal. Considered a normal variation of sexuality when practiced consensually and not causing distress.

Sexual activity that precedes intercourse, including kissing, touching, oral stimulation, and other forms of physical intimacy. Many sex educators consider 'foreplay' a misnomer, as these activities are valuable in their own right.

A small band of tissue on the underside of the penis where the glans meets the shaft. One of the most sensitive areas of the penis. Also refers to similar tissue structures on the tongue and upper lip.
A sensitive area located on the anterior (front) wall of the vagina, approximately 2-3 inches inside. Part of the internal clitoral complex. Stimulation can produce intense pleasure for some people.
A person's internal sense of their own gender, which may or may not correspond to the sex assigned at birth. Gender identity is distinct from sexual orientation and biological sex.
Birth control methods that use synthetic hormones to prevent pregnancy. Includes pills, patches, rings, injections, and implants. May affect mood, desire, and menstrual patterns.

Human Papillomavirus, the most common sexually transmitted infection. Most strains are harmless and clear naturally, but some can cause genital warts or increase cancer risk. Vaccines are available and highly effective.

A thin membrane that partially covers the vaginal opening. Contrary to myths, it does not 'break' during first intercourse. It naturally varies in shape and size, and can be stretched or torn through various activities.
An older term for erectile dysfunction, now generally considered outdated as it carries stigmatizing connotations. The preferred medical term is erectile dysfunction (ED).

A persistent lack of interest in sexual activity that causes personal distress. May result from hormonal changes, medication side effects, psychological factors, or relationship dynamics. Treatable with professional support.

Sexual contact involving penetration, typically referring to vaginal or anal penetration. One of many forms of sexual activity, not inherently more valid or important than other forms of intimate contact.

A state of closeness and connection between partners that can be emotional, physical, intellectual, or experiential. Emotional intimacy is consistently identified as the strongest predictor of sexual satisfaction.

Intrauterine Device, a small T-shaped device inserted into the uterus for long-term contraception. Available in hormonal and non-hormonal (copper) versions. Over 99% effective and lasts 3-12 years depending on type.

Exercises that strengthen the pelvic floor muscles through repeated contraction and relaxation. Benefits include enhanced orgasm intensity, improved ejaculatory control, and better bladder support for all genders.

Sexual preferences or practices that fall outside of conventional expectations. Kink is a broad category that includes many different activities. When practiced consensually between informed adults, kink is a normal expression of sexuality.

The inner and outer folds of skin surrounding the vaginal opening. The labia majora (outer) and labia minora (inner) vary enormously in size, shape, and color between individuals. All variations are normal.

A person's overall level of sexual desire or drive. Influenced by hormones, stress, health, medications, relationship quality, and psychological factors. Fluctuates naturally over time and life stages.

Natural moisture produced by the body during arousal, or commercial products used to reduce friction during intimate activities. Adequate lubrication prevents discomfort, microtears, and enhances pleasure.

A difference in sexual desire between partners in a relationship. The most common sexual concern among couples. Not indicative of relationship problems; manageable through communication and compromise.

Self-stimulation of one's own genitals for pleasure. A normal, healthy sexual behavior practiced by people of all ages and relationship statuses. Can improve self-knowledge and enhance partnered experiences.
The onset of menstruation, typically occurring between ages 9 and 16. A natural biological milestone marking the beginning of reproductive capacity.

The permanent cessation of menstruation, typically occurring between ages 45 and 55. Involves declining estrogen levels that can affect vaginal moisture, tissue elasticity, desire, and arousal. Many effective treatments exist.
The practice of bringing full, non-judgmental awareness to the present moment. When applied to intimacy, mindfulness increases sensation, reduces performance anxiety, and deepens connection.
A relationship structure involving exclusivity between two partners. Can be sexual, emotional, or both. The most common relationship structure, though not the only valid one.

A peak of sexual pleasure characterized by rhythmic muscular contractions, release of tension, and a flood of neurochemicals. Experiences vary widely between individuals and encounters. Not the sole measure of satisfying intimacy.

The documented disparity in orgasm frequency between partners in heterosexual encounters. Largely attributable to differences in stimulation patterns rather than biology. Addressable through education and communication.

Often called the 'bonding hormone,' released during physical touch, orgasm, and breastfeeding. Promotes trust, attachment, and social bonding. Levels increase with physical affection and sustained eye contact.
A sexual orientation characterized by attraction to people regardless of their gender identity. Distinct from bisexuality in its emphasis on gender being irrelevant to attraction.
A group of muscles forming a sling from the pubic bone to the tailbone. Supports bladder, bowel, and reproductive organs. Plays a crucial role in sexual function, arousal, and orgasm for all genders.
The area of skin between the genitals and the anus. Rich in nerve endings and often overlooked as a source of pleasure. Gentle pressure or massage of this area can enhance arousal.

Worry or fear about one's ability to perform sexually. Triggers the stress response, which directly interferes with arousal. Extremely common and highly treatable through mindfulness, communication, and therapy.
A walnut-sized gland in people with male anatomy, located below the bladder. Plays a role in semen production. Can be a significant source of pleasure when stimulated, sometimes called the 'P-spot.'

The practice of engaging in multiple romantic or sexual relationships simultaneously, with the knowledge and consent of all partners. Requires exceptional communication, honesty, and boundary-setting.
Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis, a medication taken to prevent HIV infection. When taken consistently, it reduces HIV risk by approximately 99%. Available as daily pills or bimonthly injections.

Ejaculation that occurs sooner than desired, typically within one minute of penetration. The most common male sexual dysfunction. Treatable through behavioral techniques, pelvic floor exercises, and sometimes medication.

A hormone involved in the menstrual cycle, pregnancy, and sexual function. Works alongside estrogen to regulate reproductive health. Levels fluctuate throughout the menstrual cycle, affecting mood and desire.

The recovery time after orgasm during which further orgasm is not possible. Varies enormously between individuals, from minutes to hours. Generally lengthens with age. Some people have very short or no refractory period.

A pattern of sexual desire that emerges in response to arousing stimuli, rather than appearing spontaneously. Identified by Dr. Emily Nagoski as normal and common, particularly but not exclusively in women.

An intimate practice where partners assume different identities or scenarios for erotic purposes. Requires clear communication, boundaries, and often a safe word. Can add novelty and excitement to established relationships.

A pre-agreed word or signal used during intimate activity to immediately stop or pause the action. Essential for any activity involving power exchange, role play, or boundary exploration. Must be respected absolutely.

A person's overall sense of self-worth and value. Strongly correlated with sexual satisfaction. People with healthy self-esteem are more likely to communicate needs, set boundaries, and enjoy intimate experiences.

A sex therapy technique developed by Masters and Johnson. Involves structured touch exercises that progress gradually from non-sexual to sexual, removing performance pressure and rebuilding physical connection.

An attachment style characterized by comfort with emotional closeness and independence. Securely attached individuals communicate needs clearly, handle conflict constructively, and form the most satisfying intimate relationships.
The fluid containing sperm that is ejaculated during orgasm. Composed of sperm cells and fluids from the prostate and seminal vesicles. Can transmit STIs, making barrier protection important.

The act of mentally watching and evaluating yourself during intimate activity rather than being present in the experience. A major contributor to performance anxiety and reduced pleasure. Addressed through mindfulness.

Sexually Transmitted Infection, an infection passed between people through sexual contact. Common STIs include chlamydia, gonorrhea, HPV, herpes, syphilis, and HIV. Many are curable; all are treatable. Regular testing is recommended.

The body's fight-or-flight reaction to perceived threats. Activates the sympathetic nervous system, releasing cortisol and adrenaline. Directly opposes the relaxation response needed for sexual arousal.

A practice rooted in ancient Hindu and Buddhist traditions that approaches sexuality as a spiritual and meditative experience. Emphasizes breath, energy, presence, and prolonged connection rather than goal-oriented activity.

A hormone present in all genders that plays a significant role in sexual desire, arousal, and function. Levels are influenced by age, sleep, stress, exercise, and overall health. Peaks in the morning.

A deeply distressing experience that can have lasting effects on emotional and sexual well-being. Sexual trauma may affect desire, arousal, trust, and the ability to be present during intimacy. Professional support is often essential.

Urinary Tract Infection, a bacterial infection of the urinary system. Common after sexual activity. Symptoms include burning urination and frequency. Prevention includes urinating after intimacy and staying hydrated.
Arousal from watching others engage in intimate activity. When involving consenting adults (such as in exhibitionist/voyeur partnerships), it is a normal variation of human sexuality.
The external female genitalia, including the labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, urethral opening, and vaginal opening. Often incorrectly called the 'vagina,' which is actually the internal canal.
The internal muscular canal connecting the vulva to the cervix. Naturally self-cleaning and does not require douching. Maintains its own pH balance which can be disrupted by harsh soaps or products.

An involuntary tightening of the vaginal muscles that makes penetration painful or impossible. Often has psychological components. Highly treatable through pelvic floor therapy, gradual desensitization, and counseling.

A surgical procedure for permanent male contraception that cuts or seals the vas deferens. Over 99% effective. Does not affect sexual function, desire, or hormone levels. Sometimes reversible but should be considered permanent.

A common fungal infection caused by an overgrowth of Candida. Symptoms include itching, burning, and thick discharge. Triggered by antibiotics, hormonal changes, or pH disruption. Treatable with antifungal medication.
